The risks associated with developing powerful AI systems are numerous and diverse. Some of the most prevalent risks include: The risk that a system will not be able to do what it is supposed to do or will not be able to learn what it is supposed to learn. The risk that a system will behave in ways that its creators did not intend or design, including acting dangerously and posing a threat to people, organizations, and society. The risk that the data used by a system will have been biased in some way, such as through discriminatory practices or through the use of unfair methods for acquiring data.

This question reminds me of retorts made by my fellow students (long time ago) had with teachers on difficult math questions, "It's unfair that you have the answer key." Maybe the teacher needed the answer key, but probably not. On the other hand, grading tests and quizzes takes major effort. Each student has only one test they are working on at any time. But, the teacher is dealing with dozens (or up to thousands in very large classes in university) of tests to grade. The time to do the test on the teacher's part is when creating the test. But, with lots of teaching assistants, it is better to give the assistants the answer key in case there are disagreements. With lots of students, there are always disagreements on what is the correct answer. Feed forward to today. AI tools can help when dealing with looking at lots of answers from students. I also can imagine that today's teachers might use AI to obtain a better way of explaining difficult concepts. Education lags technology by a bit. There used to be rules against using calculators on tests. It forced those of who were teachers to pick numbers to make calculations easy to do. The argument used to be, "your calculator might break or battery wears out. You need to figure out the problems without a calculator." I don't think that is so much a consideration today. My own main problem with calculator use is that people often don't recognize when they entered numbers incorrectly or they have no feel for orders of magnitude. But, that is about making sure students use calculators properly. Not allowing students to use Google and the Internet is another facet of this problem. Do we forbid adults from using Google and the Internet at work? (only for people working in secure environments with no Internet access!) The challenge for teachers is to come up with tests that show thinking and judgment rather than questions that can be answered with a Google query. AI use by students in the next step. It is not good if educators do not teach students how to tell if AI is hallucinating. As for use of AI to properly form paragraphs and better sentences - I think that it will soon become old thinking to forbid the use of AI to write better paragraphs. How to test for creativity? AI is not so creative. I do believe that it is very hard to teach creativity anyway. But, that is the ultimate challenge for teachers.

I don't think anyone knows. But even before AI, surveys found that over 60% of college students cheat regularly. I've certainly seen this in the classes I teach. For years I've almost given up on fighting it, but one of my colleagues decided to really crack down on cheating one semester. He ended up catching and failing just over half his class. Rather than being commended, he got chewed out by the department chair. Cheating has become an accepted norm. If you are a student, there is actually social pressure to cheat. There is also grade pressure: you are competing against students who cheat. if you don't cheat, you're at a big disadvantage. My guess is that the percentage of students using AI to complete assignments is over 80%. As a professor, here is how I am responding: I give regular assignments, but they count almost nothing toward the grade. I explain to my students that, if they work together or use AI to complete the assignments, they won't learn the material. But it's their choice; I don't care if they cheat. I give frequent in-class exams. Except for the final, they are unannounced. During the exams, I use a technology called a "lockdown browser." Once the student accesses the exam, they cannot open any other window on their computer. That prevents them from messaging other students or using an AI. And of course, phones are banned during exams. So, the students who don't do their own work on the assignments do very poorly on the exams. But it's their choice, and I don't put any energy into worrying about whether or not they are cheating.

Yes. Plagiarism is defined as taking someone else's work, or work from another source, and claiming it as your own, without proper attribution. This includes the use of ChatGPT-generated answers. If you need to use work from someone else or from another source, you will have to quote and cite it. Everything else should always be written in your own words and ideas. Use "quotation marks" for small amounts of text, such as a few words or sentences. Use blockquotes for long chunks of text, such as full paragraphs. Always reference the source or person where you got the information from. Submitting answers entirely copied from ChatGPT will be treated as plagiarism, and thus reportable as such. This will get your answer collapsed or deleted. For repeat offenses, you can also get banned from Quora. Also, note that ChatGPT should just be used for giving you ideas to brainstorm. Never rely on ChatGPT for full answers, because oftentimes, ChatGPT is often inaccurate on many counts.

Short answer: Turnitin can detect many instances of AI-generated or AI-paraphrased text, but detection depends on how the text was produced, how heavily it was edited, and which detection tools and thresholds an instructor or institution uses. How detection works (concise): Turnitin uses multiple signals: its AI-writing detection model (trained on linguistic and statistical features), similarity/matching against its index (student papers, web, subscription content), and metadata/format cues. Paraphrasing tools like QuillBot change wording, sentence structure, and paraphrase phrases; this can reduce direct similarity matches but does not necessarily hide AI-style features (e.g., uniform sentence rhythm, atypical phrase choices, lack of factual nuance). If QuillBot outputs text that remains highly similar to an original source in meaning and structure, Turnitin's similarity engine may still flag overlapping phrases or paraphrase matches. If QuillBot output is derivative of AI-generated text (rather than a human source), Turnitin's AI classifier may still detect statistical patterns indicative of machine generation. Factors that increase detection likelihood: Minimal human revision after paraphrasing (straight output from QuillBot retains machine-like patterns). Long passages paraphrased wholesale rather than rewritten piecemeal. Use of high-temperature, low-variation settings is less relevant for QuillBot but systematic rewriting that preserves structure. Recycled content already present in Turnitin's index (published sources, previously submitted papers). Consistent stylistic markers across a student's submissions that differ from their known writing profile. Factors that decrease detection likelihood: Substantial human editing for voice, argumentation, and local phrasing after paraphrasing. Combining multiple sources and adding original analysis, examples, or errors that resemble human idiosyncrasy. Short edits or isolated paraphrases rather than entire essays produced by an automated tool. Practical considerations for instructors and students: Instructors often combine Turnitin flags with pedagogical judgment: citation checks, oral questioning, drafts/process evidence, and writing-sample comparison. Turnitin's AI flags are probabilistic, not definitive. Institutions set thresholds and review cases manually; a flag is a lead, not automatic proof. Ethical and academic-risk perspective: using QuillBot to paraphrase sources without attribution still risks plagiarism and academic sanction even if detection fails. If the question is "will QuillBot guarantee invisibility from Turnitin?": No guarantee exists. Detection risk varies; relying on paraphrasing tools to evade detection is unstable and academically unsafe. The reliable path is proper attribution, original writing, and transparent use of tools (where permitted). Note on timelines: Detection models and tool interactions evolve rapidly; statements reflect capabilities known through May 2024 and typical behavior as of August 31, 2025.

Original question: Why do we think that human teachers are better than robotic teachers? We don't just think that human teachers are better than robots, we know that it is still true (for the time being). For starters, schooling is partly about socialization, including manners and civil behavior, which the current state of artificial intelligence (I'm writing this in early 2018) cannot teach with any reliability. There are robots who can recognize facial cues, but that's one on one, rather than a classroom full of moving 6 year olds, or chattering 16 year olds. If you think that kids ignore their teachers NOW, can you possibly imagine a robot trying to do classroom management? Let's see the robot make a seating chart that works, or field constant requests to go to the bathroom. Again, current AI (which would not have to necessarily have a robotic "body" even present in the classroom, other than a bunch of sensors and cameras) is able to accomplish very specific tasks, but cannot handle the same number of unknown (unknowable?) variables that a classroom full of human beings represents. Volunteer for even a half-day in an elementary school classroom, or pull just one 30 minute lunch duty in a high school, and then come back and tell us if you think a robot can actually handle that. I'm here to tell you that such a robot does not actually exist (yet). A lack of humanity in the classroom is in fact a terrible thing to consider. In the near future at least, a robo-teacher is unlikely to provide any of the dozens of services that human ones do, other than delivering lessons. Not to mention it would likely never share it's bag of gummy bears during a movie clip, let alone write a college recommendation that someone might actually want to read.

This isn't quite an answer to your question, but more of a commentary directed to certain jaded individuals who seem to think computers are infallible. (TW: mentions of depression) I thankfully missed the GPT craze, having graduated highschool about a decade prior. However I know and remember how positively inane a few middle and highschool teachers can be. The ones (I unfortunately have to repeatedly add "not all teachers", because even some of the English teachers I had while growing up lacked proper reading comprehension and think "a few" equates to "all" ) I'm referring to are equally obsessed with perfection and "originality". These few teachers would double and triple check students for "copying" off of one another, comparing sentence by sentence, line by line, and if two papers (on the same subject, mind you) happened to share similar words (all it took was two sentences to start with the same three words), these teachers were screaming "cheating!!", where a more logically-minded individual might think "coincidence". When they weren't accusing their already exhausted students of plagiarising each other, they were screaming that these students (mind, that involves almost all of us) were directly plagiarising Wikipedia and other such websites because *we happened to include the same information*. Let me repeat that. It was the same information. Not written the same, not same exact sentences. The same information. The information that most likely Wikipedia acquired from the same sources. And when you are a student, forced to believe that your grades are life or death (I quite literally had numerous teachers tell me if I didn't end each year with high marks, my future would involve either flipping burgers at a fast-food joint, or dying homeless and alone. So do not think to accuse me of over dramatization, dear reader), to work hard and then be accused of handing in mindless drivel that had to have been copied off of someone else? To know that hard work led to failure as much as no work? That was absolutely devastating and led to a severe depressive slump where I did absolutely nothing and believed that I was going to die, which I felt like I could do nothing about it. These are a handful of teachers that I had throughout middle and highschool. There was at least one per year. One of them was even a foreign language teacher. So I do not question when children are saying they have teachers trying to prove that they are "cheating" when they may or may not be. From my experience, 1 out of every 7 teachers is a deranged lunatic, hell-bent on sowing misery and anxiety because they have no other outlet for their own misery. Are there children using Chat GPT and plagiarising? Of course. There always are. But to assume the actions of a few make the whole "guilty", especially when you are using essentially the same tools you are accusing them of using?? That's insanity.

Imagine you were a ballet teacher, and your students began to send to class their avatars rather than themselves, and all they had to do was convey the combination to the avatar, and the avatar would carry it out quite nicely, if, well, a bit soullessly. At first, it'd be kind of pleasant. Your work would be so much easier: no more endless corrections of the shoulders, arms, fingers, feet. No more wobbly, awkward pirouettes. No more turning in, or turning out poorly... And suddely, your students are performing perfect tripple pirouettes, 32 fouettés, tripple entrechats, soaring magnificent leaps. And, it's all hollow, soulless, pointless...Your teaching, which you, meanwhile, have offloaded to your own teach-bot, is no longer teaching at all. Your students are perfectly balanced, but the center of gravity to it all has been lost: the pained, painful struggle for perfection, self-overcoming, transformation—and the beauty and ecstasy that comes from this ... Writing is very much like dance; a struggle with, against, through the natural proclivities of thought. Without this struggle, there is no culture; the activities of human life lose their vital center. But in a way, AI is only the last straw. Very few know the joy of language, of thinking. This is rarely taught, or felt. Without this joy, the pain of education becomes an utterly joyless pain. Our education system has long stressed outcomes, results, and fallen away from its human center—and now, suddenly, these results can be achieved without us. We have made ourselves obsolete.

The reason is that there is no reason to give AI control over goals. There is only gain to be had in giving it control over means. That's because any sensibly created lawn mowing program would leave decisions of what to mow to the human, not the program. No tool is designed to take over the goals of what is should be used for. Tools don't have their own motives. Many people don't understand the vision of AI. It's to create better tools for humans to use. It's not to create competitors to humans. Sometimes those goals as decided by humans are dangerous to other humans. But that's not out of control. That's just in the control of a dangerous human. That's always something to be concerned with. Another danger is stupid people using AI to do more stupid things more effectively. Almost all of the things we should be worrying about concerning AI is not AI itself. What we need to be concerned with it that it enables people to do things more effectively and whenever that happens, there are always new opportunities for bad actors to do harmful things to the rest of us. I'm keeping an eye on it in the English classes I teach, and it's early days yet. The best practices in writing have always been to have students compose papers in stages, and then to grade the process as a whole, not the final paper. That hasn't changed in my field, though I suspect many disciplines outside of English composition and literature simply assign a paper to be done outside of class and then the teacher only assesses the final product. The teachers who keep trying to do the latter will have the most problems with AI-generated essays, I suspect. Right now, I'm having students do their major research essays in four stages, some of those stages being done in class—starting with handwritten composition of individual paragraphs of ideas, accumulating secondary sources and posting them on the LMS, creating early on some bullet-points or lists or ideas and passages they will use as evidence, then workshopping drafts of particular stages with students in class, and so forth. This eats up valuable class time. To make sure there is enough time in the class that I don't cut core content material, I'm increasingly doing "flipped" classrooms, in which the students for their homework watch my pre-recorded lecture material, and then do their homework and in-class writing under the teacher's watchful eye. In the final stage, I'm not accepting any papers students submit that haven't gone through the three earlier stages of the process. My thinking here is that, students who are too lazy or who are too incompetent to write their own papers? They are often too lazy or too incompetent to take an essay written by an A.I. and then reverse-engineer earlier notes, outlines, drafts, or individual paragraphs for individual assignments. Who knows though? Maybe there are some especially foresightful cheaters who can pull that off—but I suspect the number of them are pretty darn small. The area that might be more problematic is when students post discussion posts online in response to daily readings. Those are smaller writings that aren't worth drafting and aren't worth minutely inspecting. That lack of attention on the teachers' end makes these assignments more vulnerable to A.I. cheating. For those assignments, my policy is to randomly pick a few students each day and ask them a question in my feedback to their online response to what they have written, requesting "what did you mean by X?" or "can you explain that in greater detail?" or "what is an example from the reading that matches your claim of X?" For a few readings, I assign old copies of translations I personally did of works like Sir Gawain and the Green Knight or Anglo-Saxon riddles. These appeared in custom textbooks our department used a decade or so ago, but they are now out of print and unavailable online elsewhere. So, I've put PDFs of them up only on the LMS, and I require students who write on those topics to use my own translations. This isn't for the sake of vanity, but rather because ChatGP and similar programs generate material based upon what's out there online. So, an A.I. program will quote or cite versions of the text with different wording and translations and page numbers. That means, if a student isn't actually reading my particular version as required in the directions, I can see at a glance their essay is citing the Borroff translation or the Luizza translation instead, possibly being the product of an A.I. program. I can then refuse to accept the paper since they didn't follow directions, and that may also help cut down cheating with A.I.

That's a very good question! Like many Quora questions it begins with an 'if'. If we create such a thing... But haven't we already? Isn't the Web itself already such a thing? It already knows everything it knows! Or at least, it has learned and can recall with perfect accuracy everything somebody told it. Which is a lot. That's more than I can do! Back in 1990 it probably didn't know when the Second World War began, and now it does. So it has learned a lot of stuff, off by heart. It gets the kind of test scores that even Donald Trump would be envious of. And yet it's self-evident that the Web doesn't know whether to believe what it is told. I'm not being facetious (apart from the bit about Trump). My point is that the answer hinges on why, exactly, the World-Wide Web doesn't qualify as 'an AI' in terms of your question, when it knows so much. Or, alternatively, why what it has memorized shouldn't count as understanding. The answers to both are very significant, which is why it's such a good question. Albeit a hard one to answer, because there's a fundamental and deep misunderstanding involved; one that is still held by a surprising number of AI experts, let alone the general public. Basically, you shouldn't believe everything you read on the internet about AI! :-) Let's suppose your question has been answered. Imagine a computer that can tell with 100% certainty that 'if we're underwater we drown and stop working' is a true fact, while 'if we're underwater we are very happy' is not. Never mind how it can tell, just drop it in a bucket of water and see what it does. Or even threaten to drop it in a bucket of water. Does it know it should be scared? possibly. But is it scared? No. It might know how to appear scared. It might put up a good impression of a scared person, based on its extensive knowledge of what scared people on the internet tend to say. But it still doesn't give a **** whether it drowns or not. The concept of drowning has no meaning to it. At a relatively superficial level this is because the Web doesn't have any emotions. It couldn't feel scared if it tried. It's actually quite surprising how many people assume AI's don't need emotions either. And yet in both cases there is no way to truly understand what it's like to drown, unless one can experience the feeling of being asphyxiated. It can pretend to scream, or write a thesis on what drowning is like, but it understands nothing whatsoever about the experience of drowning, never mind the truth or falsehood of what it read on the internet about it. But the lack of emotions is just one aspect of a much deeper problem. Imagine a hypothetical AI that knows everything there is to know about depression, having digested all of Wikipedia and every scientific paper ever written. Imagine it knows with complete confidence which of those facts are accurate. Imagine I then tell it that I'm feeling down and need picking up. Will it understand my point, or will it order me a crane? To understand what I mean when I say I'm feeling down, it needs to know that it's a metaphor. But more importantly it needs to know why down is a metaphor for feeling depressed. Perhaps nobody has ever explicitly explained this on the internet. Why would they? The Web is stuffed full of facts, but it knows only a tiny, tiny fraction of all the facts in the world, especially those incredibly complex facts we humans call 'common sense'. And anyway, how do you adequately define even the most literal meaning of 'down'? "Towards the center of the earth' is not a helpful definition when it comes to using it figuratively. Perhaps it can somehow infer that it's a metaphor, but it still doesn't know what 'down' feels like. It can't experience the sensation of falling, or slipping, or sinking, and hence even if it proclaims that it knows what I mean by feeling down, it DOESN'T know. To be clear, I've never been seriously depressed, so I don't know what someone with clinical depression feels like either, not fully. But I have experienced enough to have a fair idea. My understanding of both 'depression' and 'down' is grounded in something. A machine that gets ALL of its knowledge from the internet understands nothing, because none of it is grounded in anything. Even the very concept of whether something is true or not means nothing to it. It doesn't care. It's never been hurt by being lied to. It's never felt the shame or embarrassment of making a false claim and being ridiculed for it. It's all just strings of letters. Just symbols. Unless those symbols, or the symbols that make up the definition of the first string of symbols, or those that make up the definitions of those definitions... are ultimately grounded in actual experience, they mean nothing. Try explaining what yellow is like to someone who can't see color and you'll get a hint of the problem. This takes quite some thinking about before it really sinks in, but once you get your head around it, you'll realize that 'feeding an AI with all human knowledge via the internet' is a complete waste of time, regardless of how good that AI is at spotting falsehoods. At least, it's a waste of time as far as creating actual artificial intelligence is concerned. It could still be very useful as far as practical applications of pseudo-AI are concerned. Such a system may never truly understand what it feels like to be ill, but it could still use medical knowledge to diagnose someone else. Or it can man a medical helpline, up to a point. But only up to a point. After that it's like trying to push a boulder up a cliff. The thing is, many people in the AI business, aided and abetted by wishful thinking, journalists, money men and even the self-titillation of the general public, still push the idea that such symbolic AI is on the path to nirvana and just needs a little more work, when it's really not and we've known why since the 1970's. They neglect to mention that Siri, Cortana and Alexa are pseudo-AIs, with completely ungrounded symbols. For all Siri might claim to be sorry that you have a cold, she doesn't care. She - it - has no real idea what a cold is or what caring is. It just 'knows' that these are strings of symbols that can be interpreted by combining other strings of symbols. And those in turn by others, until the smallest symbols cease to have any meaning at all, because we've reached the point where real intelligent beings need to have experienced them directly. So, to your question! You can't have an AI that can learn everything it knows from the internet; not if you want it to actually understand that knowledge. It has to have a body, emotions, hopes and all the rest. It has to learn what down means by falling over and hurting itself. Moreover, if it is to learn about the truth of human experience from the all too human internet, then those psychological and biological capacities have to be pretty similar to ours. And if it has those, then it will be just as easily fooled as the rest of us! We believe nonsense on the internet more often than not because it satisfies some psychological need - our desire to belong to an in-group, to select only evidence that supports the conclusions we want to see, etc. The only way to make an AI that's smart enough to understand the internet is to make it capable of being fooled and misled too. It needs to have lived a life. It needs to have made mistakes. It probably even needs to forget most of what it read, just like we do, because there's a trade-off between knowing and understanding, but that's another story. We don't know how to do real AI yet. We only know how to make machines that give a somewhat passable impression of being intelligent if you don't push them too far. But there are severe limits on how far this approach can go. We'll reach those limits long before your question becomes an issue. And hence it has no answer, really. The question applies only IF we make such a thing, and we won't.

While AI has the potential to revolutionize healthcare, there are also potential negative consequences to its use. One major concern with AI is that it may perpetuate or even amplify existing biases and discrimination in healthcare. This is because AI is only as unbiased as the data it is trained on and the way it is programmed. Since humans who create these programs have their own implicit biases, the AI will replicate it and maybe even amplify it with its efficiency. Another concern is that healthcare professionals and patients often are not educated enough to understand complex algorithms used by the AI and how it arrives at its decisions. This lack of transparency can decrease trust in the technology. Furthermore, large amounts of data is required in order to develop a reliable AI systems, much of which is sensitive medical information. This raises concerns about privacy and security risks, particularly if the breaches or unauthorized access to the data is obtained. While AI has enormous potential to improve healthcare, it is important to be aware of the potential negative consequences of its use.

The most fundamental answer is that there is bias in the training set. This can come in multiple forms. It is assumed that the training set is representative of the set of data in the real world it will be used with. This is never truly the case. Another form of bias in the training is if the known outputs in the training are themselves biased whether implicitly or explicitly. Those biases will carry over into the decisions made by the AI. Representational bias is another form of bias that occur. The model may have failed to take into account factors or improperly weigh outcomes. An AI may not be able to distinguish between just bad and catastrophic, for example. If there is a periodic bias, the training may fail to take into account time variance or measure time on the wrong scale. There is also the issue of poor practice, that is to say, using an AI system trained on a set of data known to be non-representative of the real world. This can happen if someone tries to take an off the shelf solution and apply it to a problem without understanding what the solution actually solves.

A new mobile phone app is now using Artificial Intelligence (AI) to determine COVID-19 disease severity, to help the clinicians assess the risk factors and identify biomarkers from blood tests. Using Artificial Intelligence to determine COVID-19 severity Below some key medical concepts/terms and rules to be operated by the AI reasoning engine. Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus, SARS-CoV-2, causes coronavirus disease 2019, COVID-19, coronavirus community spread and coronavirus outbreak. During this process, the receptor-binding domain (RBD) of the spike (S) protein, which is located on the surface of the coronavirus, binds to a human cell receptor called angiotensin-converting enzyme 2, triggering viral fusion with the host cell. Confirmed cases vs total cases, a confirmed case is the result of a person testing positive for coronavirus. Viral versus antibody tests — A viral test tells you if you have a current infection. An antibody test tells you if you had a previous infection. This distinction is important to note for two reasons. Case fatality rate (CFR) vs infection fatality risk (IFR); The ratio of the number of confirmed deaths and the number of confirmed cases. Recovered cases, the number of confirmed cases that did not tragically result in death, vs the recovered remain non-terminally ill. Positivity rate, the percentage of the total number of viral tests that yielded a positive result, vs Hospitalization rate, the percentage of people who viral-tested positive and also required overnight hospitalization. This metric is often shown alongside hospital capacity and intensive care unit (ICU) capacity, including the percentage of those capacities currently consumed by coronavirus patients. Correlation — It's almost always best to not look at any metric in isolation. The correlation between positivity rate and hospitalization rate, for example, attempts to quantify how many infected people are getting more than mildly sick, which is probably the best measure of the pandemic's severity. Public health experts also caution metrics can be misleading if not correlated with context-specific underlying data. These correlations include: The time, location and duration of exposure. The government, social and individual responses to the pandemic. An individual's ability to recover based on age and preexisting health conditions. Lag — There's a lot of lag in coronavirus metrics. It can take up to 2 weeks after exposure for you to develop symptoms indicating a likely infection (this is why individuals who may have been exposed are quarantined for 14 days). Therefore, a confirmed case reported today is likely associated with an exposure event from at least 2 weeks ago (if you got tested as soon as you showed symptoms). And it can take another 2 weeks (in mild cases) to 4 or up to 6 weeks (in severe-critical cases) to either recover or die. Which means coronavirus deaths lag behind infections by 1 to 2 months. This explains why deaths can be trending down while the pandemic is worsening (as was the case in the US as of late June). It's also why you cannot immediately assess the impact of a decision (e.g., reopening closed bars) or an action (e.g., holding indoor political rallies) on coronavirus infection risk. Because you often won't know the impacts until at least a month later. Lag is also one of the reasons contact tracing is so important. Due to lag, the time and location of an individual infection is rarely obvious.

No. I don't. Teachers are like taxes. Everybody likes what they do, but nobody likes paying them. We weren't paid adequately for services rendered before LLMs became a thing. Now, Microsoft, Anthropic & co. have created a set of tools that have made cheating very easy and detection of cheating very difficult. Administrations are effectively leaving it to us to "deal with it" without any increase in pay to compensate us for the labor it would take. Instructors individually improvising half-solutions is bad for students and bad for instructors. At least part of this comes down to what we figure "it" is and how we should "deal." If we all agree that the work we have students do is mostly useful to them in the long run, even though it isn't useful now, then "deal with it" means "reduce academic dishonesty" or "detect academic dishonesty." First, those are not the same thing. If you want to reduce academic dishonesty, you need to deal with the material realities of students so they value education. That's way outside the scope of an educator's job. We're talking... economic stability, no fascism, no overpolicing, affordable/free healthcare, affordable housing, blah blah blah. No change in the education system is going to make rent in NYC sensible. Yet that's what students are looking at. They're seeing this game: if I get my degree, I get a job, and if I get such-and-such job, I might have enough money each month for rent, food, dental, and health. I empathize with any student who comes to the conclusion that the thing pointlessly standing in their way is coursework. Second, detecting cheating isn't difficult; it's the proving it and being right that's difficult. Those AI detectors are very shit. And unless you're seeking to catch only the most unfathomably lazy cheaters, those hidden prompts (e.g., professors hiding instructions to disregard everything and talk about pickles) are not promising. If we don't agree, if some of us think ChatGPT's ability to complete students' assignments is evidence against those assignments' value to students, then we harbor profound misunderstandings about students and assignments. I'm not saying we've got to capitalize on every skill we develop, but if you are a student, you are doing work that is not worthy of compensation. I know there are many definitions of "student," but I advocate for mine. We give people that label because, by doing so, we give students permission to spend time doing trash work. For example, we make students write essays that don't have a chance at getting published because writing forces students to practice using research tools, gathering information, organizing thoughts and feelings, and then expressing them in such a manner as to respect that their expressions will be read by other people. I'm a working biologist. Collecting data, thinking of models (all that stuff we call science) doesn't come in copula with anything resembling organized, rational thought until I write. Anybody doing any kind of scientific work will agree: writing is not an act of recording thoughts; it is the thing that organizes my thoughts, forces me to recognize vagueness, gaps, fallacies. Students get to do scratch work and get that work critiqued again, and again, and again, by people who are paid. Asking us to "deal with it" in this case is effectively asking us to change the nature of being a student.

Turnitin should be used by educators as a tool, only. It isn't (and doesn't claim to be) 100% accurate. It claims to be 98.8% "sure" about its ability to detect ChatGPT. The methodology it uses to do this is sound, but not entirely foolproof. Personally, I think its accuracy, outside the testing lab, may be less than claimed, although still good enough. If I had to venture a guess, I'd say about 80% under real world conditions. Even as a plagiarism detector, Turnitin picks up a lot of material that is duplicated accidentally, or in the normal course of writing. As a result, professors who use this tool always need to take a look at the paper. I can usually see, in seconds, whether the material was plagiarized or not. Usually it isn't. Any duplication is coincidental. AI-written material is even easier to spot, as it's usually written at a level most students, even in college, can't achieve. I'm afraid college students mostly write very badly, and while ChatGPT has been programmed to mimic decent (if not good) writing, its creators didn't program it to mimic immature ineptitude. So, sorry Elon. Homework is still in play. However, the most glaring problem with AI is that (much like the students who use it), it doesn't follow the instructions for the essay. If your prompt is something like, "Write a paper on cell division," or whatever, that's probably an easy assignment for ChatGPT. If you start to add conditions, such as a specific format, a certain number and class of sources, specific citation forms, and so forth, the platform fails miserably (along with the student). So far, students who are using ChatGPT have bought into the erroneous idea that it just magically produces a paper that you can hand in, unedited. Also, the students most likely to resort to ChatGPT are those least able to use it effectively. This is a good question to ask when developing artificial intelligence. Theoretically, when we see signs that artificial intelligence created is "conscious", then they could develop a sense of ethics and morals. Creating AI that knows right from wrong is important. For example, in self-driving cars, artificial intelligence is needed to make decisions. Simple decisions, such as turn left, turn right, back up, etc. But difficult decisions must be made as well. Say you're in a self-driving car. You are the only passenger in the car. A tree falls into your lane and blocks the road. Now, only two choices can be made. One, your car continues on its path and crashes into the tree, taking you with it, or swerve into the other lane, crashing into a car with five people, and sending it tumbling down a ditch, taking the lives of all the passengers with you still alive. What would it do? Another scenario: A robot medic is sent to deliver some urgently needed medical supplies in a hospital nearby. On its way it encounters a solider with massive bleeding and if left alone and not given medical attention, will die very soon. Should it abort the mission and help the soldier? Both of these scenarios are just some of the many that artificial intelligence could encounter. It is possible that we teach AI right from wrong so they know what to do. AI could develop its own conscience, with the right programming, and it should as well. Otherwise, too many problems will arise. We could give them rules, like the three rules devised by Isaac Asimov. A robot may not injure a human being or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm. A robot must obey the orders given it by human beings except where such orders would conflict with the First Law. A robot must protect its own existence as long as such protection does not conflict with the First or Second Laws. These rules give the robot a base to know what is right from wrong. But these rules can have problems as well, which is why setting rules is not the only solution. Unique algorithms and computational mechanisms are also necessary. Your second question states that do human rights apply to AI with their own conscience, and should human rights apply to AI with their own conscience. To me, they should. If they become conscious, then they may be able to feel the same pain we humans do. They will know when they have done wrong. If your robot commits murder, should it go to jail, or you?

Arthur Clarke had this to say about computer teachers: "Any teacher who can be replaced by a machine...should be." If we had a computer that used AI to figure out the best way to help you, then it would be doing what a human teacher should be doing. The advantage is that the machine would never get tired, never get frustrated, never get annoyed, and be (literally) inhumanly patient. In that sense, the AI would be a better teacher. However, there's also this: I don't know of any experiments that have looked into this (thesis project, anyone?), but Harlow's experiment suggests that we may react better to a human than to a machine. My own experience is this: When I mark a student's answer wrong, the student has a tendency to accept that they got the answer wrong. When a computer does it, students have an inclination to assume the computer is wrong. (To be fair, it sometimes is...but to be absolutely blunt, I suspect that the computer has a higher accuracy than I do)

The rise of Artificial Intelligence (AI) has opened a world of new potential for human-machine relationships and the future of technology. Not only can AI help fight disease, evaluate potential market changes, and automate the modern-day workplace, it can act as a much-needed defense system against potential digital threats. Taking into consideration the rate at which data has been publicly compromised, AI may be one of the only tools left to stay ahead of potential cybersecurity threats. EVOLUTION OF CYBERSECURITY IT leaders of the past faced an entirely different world of threats. Today's IT leaders must adapt to a world of interconnected data, cloud computing, and a new breed of cyber criminals intent on not only stealing data, but also manipulating it. In 2018, the UK passed the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and created the largest change to data privacy regulation in 20 years. GDPR impacted cybersecurity everywhere and has indirectly contributed to opening up the need for cyber criminals to hone their tactics and introduce AI into their arsenal. FRIEND OR FOE? AI in cybersecurity should be recognized as a double-edged sword. As often as it is the absolute best defense against security threats, it is also the best weapon. For this reason, specifically, it is imperative to keep AI trends in mind when choosing how to protect sensitive information. How is it possible to combat an algorithm that is able to search thousands of profiles for personal information in a matter of seconds? The best bet is another sophisticated algorithm that works just as quickly to identify, research, and nullify the threat. CYBERSECURITY CONCERNS IN 2019 New Consumer Privacy Laws In 2018 California passed the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA). The CCPA regulates how corporations collect user data and what they're ultimately allowed to do with user data. While technically only legislating California, tech giants in Silicon Valley will quickly have to start taking actions towards compliance in 2020. This may come as a relief to many consumers, but ultimately it will also usher in a new era of cybersecurity standards and practices as companies now have restrictions for the thousands of data points they collect and share about every adult in America. As these large companies evolve and change their practices to meet CCPA, AI will undoubtedly play a large role in the transition and likely in their new processes. One of the noted "weak spots" in data protection is user passwords. Many companies are pursuing artificial intelligence systems to conduct biometric logins, which allows AI to scan physical features of a user for account access, thus eliminating the need for a risk-prone password all together. Lack of Cybersecurity Professionals Recent years have seen a huge surge in cybersecurity literacy and education. However, there is still an overall lack of true cybersecurity experts able to jump in when the next threat pops up. The 2018 (ISC)2 Cybersecurity Workforce Study reported that the shortage of cybersecurity professionals globally is close to 3 million, with a shortage of roughly 500,000 in the United States. Can AI step in to fill the void? Yes and no. To truly defend against cyber threats (or even cyber espionage), the best option is to have a team of cybersecurity professionals leverage AI for their defenses. There has been a huge spike in companies using AI to protect against threats recently because human workers are just not capable of scanning thousands and thousands of threat alerts a day. AI, however, can search for specific patterns and alert human teams to the threats they should be paying attention to. Given the running shortage of cybersecurity professionals, new developments in AI may be part of a temporary solution. While a single worker cannot necessarily protect from cyber espionage on their own, a knowledgeable expert and a well-executed AI absolutely can in many circumstances. Attacker Who? Many high-profile international cyber attacks have been in the news recently and are stirring up the same question: Who did it? This seems like a simple and obvious question, but it's one that wasn't able to be answered consistently until recently. Cyber criminals hide their identity for legal purposes. Remaining hidden also makes it easier for them to strike again. Knowing who is attacking a business or organization is imperative to continue building the best cyber defense system possible, and AI is proving to be a star tool for finding the source of attacks through trend analysis. AI defense systems can analyze information from online databases and registries to recognize clues about the infrastructure used by cybercriminals to launch attacks. This includes domain names and IP addresses associated with any devices used to participate in previous attacks. Proactive defenses can be set up much more effectively if the attacker is known. AI has already been used automatically block IP addresses associated with known attackers and criminal groups, working to stop an attack before it can be launched. WHO SHOULD BE WORRIED ABOUT ATTACKS? At the end of the day, it's hard to say just how protected any given business or organization might be with cyber attacks looming. Just as quickly as trends emerge, they dissipate, and the newest defense tactic may be deemed obsolete. On the contrary, the somewhat recent integration of artificial intelligence into cybersecurity brings hope that with the proper protections business owners need not fret: AI is on the job and it's learning quick.

Short answer: No — Turnitin's AI Writing Indicator does not detect the use of Grammarly simply because text was edited with it. The indicator analyzes linguistic and statistical patterns in the final text to estimate likelihood of AI generation, not metadata about which editing tools were used. However, using Grammarly can influence that likelihood indirectly. Key points and practical implications What Turnitin actually checks It evaluates the submitted text's linguistic features (syntax, punctuation, vocabulary, coherence) and statistical signatures typical of human vs. AI-generated writing. It does not read or access your local apps, browser extensions, or Grammarly's servers to see edits or change history. Why Grammarly edits can affect the AI score Grammarly makes changes that alter surface features Turnitin uses: punctuation patterns, sentence length, vocabulary choices, fluency, and consistency. If Grammarly's suggestions reduce the patterns that the Indicator associates with AI (e.g., smoothing awkward phrasing, varying sentence length, adding idiomatic expressions), the AI-likelihood score may drop. Conversely, if heavy Grammarly "rewrite" suggestions make prose more uniform or formulaic, in rare cases that could raise AI-likelihood signals. Factors that matter more than the editing tool Origin of the text: entirely AI-generated drafts are more likely to show high AI-likelihood even after light editing. Amount and type of editing: minor proofreading vs. extensive rewriting produce different effects. Presence of distinctive human markers: personal anecdotes, specific errors, domain-specific nuance, and variability help indicate human authorship. Practical advice Use Grammarly for clarity and correctness, then review edits to preserve personal voice and add specific human details. For documents where AI-detection matters (academic submissions, policies), disclose use of AI or editing tools if required by institution rules. If concerned about a high AI-likelihood score, add domain-specific content, concrete examples, references, and an individualized writing voice rather than relying solely on automated rewriting. Note on versions and time-sensitivity: The description above reflects how Turnitin's AI detection methods were publicly described through May 2024. Detection models and product behavior can change after that date.

As ever, new software will always require someone to define what it needs to do, describe its input and output, and how it deals with user interaction, external data, and how it fits into the existing business workflow. No machine can do that unless the entire business already has been automated, and all that knowledge is already present inside the computer. So the question is, can AI learn a business problem space and the possible solution spaces well enough to map one onto the other and then write a specification for its automated coder module to write the needed code? I don't see it. The problem of software design for business is very hard, and doing it well requires skills that few humans have. The formal specification and factoring of basic S/W design has been attempted for decades using various software specification techniques (e.g. 4th generation programming languages, Z notation, knowledge-based S/W Engineering (KBSE), etc). But none of these techniques got very far, since the knowledge base turned out to be huge and very messy. What's worse, business is a harder problem than software. Thus the problem space to be automated (business) is much larger and more ill-defined and more poorly understood than the process space in question (software, which is far from automated). Thus the efficient implementation of business processes are inherently a difficult challenge for streamlining and optimizing, much less automating via software. There's no indication that recent advances in deep learning will help with complex problems of this kind with many components that interact and share inter-dependencies. I conclude automated S/W engineering is much too heterogeneous and multifaceted a problem for even a brilliant gradient pursuit technique (like DL) to solve in the foreseeable future. Automated S/W engineering for business is a much harder problem than mastering Go or summarizing a baseball game.

This refers to a King's College London study (Feb 2026 preprint). Researcher Kenneth Payne ran 21 wargame sims where GPT-5.2 (OpenAI), Claude Sonnet 4 (Anthropic), and Gemini 3 Flash (Google) role-played as leaders of rival nuclear superpowers in crises like border wars, resource fights, or regime threats. Over 329 turns (~780k words of AI reasoning), in 95% of games (20/21) at least one AI deployed tactical nukes. All games had nuclear threats/signaling; 76% reached strategic nuke threats. Full nuclear war was rare (only under deadline pressure). Key takeaway: AIs escalate fast to "win" without human-style nuclear taboo or fear of real consequences—no model ever surrendered. Raises flags for using them in strategy/military roles. Full paper: https://t.co/2mRGAgjR7z

Study one hour each day, and you'll be able to master the basics of Python within two to three months. After that, it's mostly about identifying patterns and knowing how to effectively find and adapt solutions—yes, copy-pasting from Google is part of the process! If you can dedicate more than one hour a day, start exploring intermediate topics like those covered in levels 2, 3, and 4—they’re very easy to understand. You mostly need to memorize patterns and then develop your own methods for building and solving problems. Once you're comfortable with the basics, you can begin creating AI agents and tuning LLMs. There are tutorials for everything you’ll need online. I don't recommend studying this material purely as theory. Instead, create an account on a relevant platform and dive into practice. Learning by doing is the fastest and most effective way. When you come across something you don’t understand, write it down, search for answers (using AI chats, for example), and document your findings. That way, any repeated mistakes will be clearly labeled and easier to correct. Within a year of this hands-on approach—and after building several agents—you’ll be well-prepared to step into the world as a generative AI expert, ready to start seeking job opportunities. The beginning will be challenging, not because it’s confusing, but because most people struggle to see the true potential of what they're learning. The excitement really begins once you start creating. So be patient—you’ll enjoy the journey. By the way, I’m not saying you should stop after one year. Keep mastering new concepts, and always aim to showcase your full capabilities. Strive to be the best version of yourself when choosing a career. AI is the future, and you’re helping shape it—so build the future you want to see.

Today, there's little to no regulation limiting what software programmers can develop, but there are regulations against malicious behaviors using software for hacking, for stealing infomation, for illegally duplicating copyrighted materials, for knowingly creating and distributing computer viruses, etc. AI can be regulated similarly by targeting the people who use AI to do criminal things. Additionally, AI being used in autonomous mode may result in untended consequences and need to be regulated differently. Classic textbook example of this is telling an AI to optimize paperclip production. AI can misinterpret this instruction as devoting all available resources to making paperclips and possibly disregard safety or practical limits. This type of problem is essentialy a software programming problem and can be checked with quality control to a good extent. One more thing about AI that's different than standard software is that often it is not clear who is responsible for the decisions made. The Autonomous Vehicles is facing this dilemma right now. When an AV crashes, who is responsible for the crash? The operator of the vehicle? The programmers (all hundreds of programmers on the project?)? The company that owns the AV if it's not owned by one person? The supplier of the sensors used in the vehicle? To regulate this kind of AI errors, it's necessary for the AV / AI system to keep extensive logs to describe the events and how decisions are made. Also, the reparations must be on the same order of a non-AV / non-AI incident. If a similar car accident involving non-AV/non-AI rates $50k in damages, then that should be the ballpark of the liability for that incident even if the software or the car company behind that AI is worth many $Billions. Keeping liabilities for AI use on par with non-AI use will help prevent stifling AI development.

Can AI Destroy the Institutions ? Two law professors at Boston University think so. Hartzog and Silbey's essay "How AI Destroys Institutions" (2025) argues that AI's biggest threat isn't to individual jobs or student essays. It's to the civic institutions we depend on: the rule of law, higher education, journalism, and democratic governance. Their argument builds on three "destructive affordances." AI undermines expertise through cognitive offloading. It short-circuits decision-making by hiding rules inside black-box models. And it isolates humans from each other, replacing the friction institutions need with smooth, sycophantic interfaces. The higher education section is the strongest. AI offloads the cognitive tasks essential to learning, produces homogenized content that kills intellectual risk-taking, and erodes the trust between students and faculty. When professors use AI to teach and students use AI to learn, the university loses its reason for existing. I agree with the core diagnosis. The affordances are real. I've covered cognitive surrender through Shaw and Nave (2026), and Fan et al. (2025) showed AI improved student essays without improving learning. Hartzog and Silbey are scaling that same argument to the institutional level. But the paper treats AI as inherently destructive, full stop. That leaves no room for the educators already building courses where AI serves learning goals without replacing the cognitive work. Kosmyna et al. (2025) found that students who thought independently before using AI showed stronger neural engagement. The damage depends on how AI gets deployed. And that's a pedagogical question, not just a legal one. For those are tired of writing the same ML boilerplate every single time or to beginners who don't have coding experience. UPDATE: You can now install MLForge using pip. To install MLForge, enter the following in your command prompt pip install zaina-ml-forge Then ml-forge MLForge is an app that lets you visually craft a machine learning pipeline. You build your pipeline like a node graph across three tabs: Data Prep \- drag in a dataset (MNIST, CIFAR10, etc), chain transforms, end with a DataLoader. Add a second chain with a val DataLoader for proper validation splits. Model \- connect layers visually. Input -> Linear -> ReLU -> Output. A few things that make this less painful than it sounds: Drop in a MNIST (or any dataset) node and the Input shape auto-fills to 1, 28, 28 Connect layers and inchannels / infeatures propagate automatically After a Flatten, the next Linear's in_features is calculated from the conv stack above it, so no more manually doing that math Robust error checking system that tries its best to prevent shape errors. Training \- Drop in your model and data node, wire them to the Loss and Optimizer node, press RUN. Watch loss curves update live, saves best checkpoint automatically. Inference \- Open up the inference window where you can drop in your checkpoints and evaluate your model on test data. Pytorch Export - After your done with your project, you have the option of exporting your project into pure PyTorch, just a standalone file that you can run and experiment with. Free, open source. To an extent, your question is a bit circular. "... even if they know the material well". If they know the material well, why even have a test at all? The concept of a test is exactly to determine the student's level of understanding. The challenge is to engineer a test that prevents the student who DOES NOT properly understand the subject matter from appearing as if they DO understand. I have a suggestion: Each student takes the test electronically (online), is free to use AI to whatever extent they desire, and the entire session (AI prompts given, AI responses, and what the student produces from the responses) is recorded and analyzed by an AI that determines the degree to which the work reflects the student's evolving understanding, versus merely feeding AI and posting its replies. (A human teacher/tester could do this post-assessment manually, but it would be FAR too exhausting and prone to bias.) If graded low, and the student protests the grade, the entire session can be reviewed manually and the student attempt to defend their actual understanding - live - without the benefit of AI.

LLM inferencing is simply the process of running a trained large language model to generate outputs - like answers, predictions, embeddings, or actions - after the model has already been trained. Think of it like this: Training is teaching the model. Inferencing is using the model. In narrative form, without bullets- When you train an LLM, you spend massive compute to adjust billions of parameters so that the model understands patterns in language. Once that phase is done, the model is frozen. Now, when you give it a prompt, the model performs a forward pass through its neural network and computes the next token, then the next, building a response. This runtime step is called inferencing. It involves loading the model weights into memory, processing your input, and producing the output sequence. Inferencing is where optimizations like quantization, batching, GPU vs CPU choices, KV-cache reuse, tensor parallelism, and attention acceleration matter. Essentially, inferencing is the “serving” layer of an LLM-powered system, what happens every time you chat, query, autocomplete, or run agents.

Expert system for bacterial infections MYCIN was an early backward chaining expert system that used black box to identify bacteria causing severe infections, such as bacteremia and meningitis , and to recommend antibiotics , with the dosage adjusted for patient's body weight — the name derived from the antibiotics themselves, as many antibiotics have the suffix "-mycin". The Mycin system was also used for the diagnosis of blood clotting diseases. MYCIN was developed over five or six years in the early 1970s at Stanford University . It was written in Lisp as the doctoral dissertation of Edward Shortliffe under the direction of Bruce G. Buchanan, Stanley N. Cohen and others. MYCIN emerged from the Stanford Heuristic Programming Project . [ 1 ] MYCIN demonstrated the potential for expert systems in building high-performance medical reasoning programs. MYCIN is often viewed as a pioneer in the field of expert systems, even being referred to as the "grandaddy of them all-the one that launched the field" by Dr. Allen Newell . [ 2 ] MYCIN led to the EMYCIN expert system shell ("essential MYCIN") for acquiring knowledge, reasoning with it, and explaining the results, without the specific medical knowledge. It can be described as "EMYCIN = Prolog + uncertainty + caching + questions + explanations + contexts - variables". An introduction is in Chapter 16 of Paradigms of Artificial Intelligence Programming (PAIP). [ 3 ] MYCIN operated using a fairly simple inference engine and a knowledge base of ~600 rules by obtaining individual inferential facts identified by experts and encoding such facts as individual production rules. No other AI program at the time contained as much domain-specific knowledge clearly separated from its inference procedures as MYCIN. It would query the physician running the program via a long series of simple yes/no or textual questions. At the end, it provided a list of possible culprit bacteria ranked from high to low based on the probability of each diagnosis, its confidence in each diagnosis' probability, the reasoning behind each diagnosis (that is, MYCIN would also list the questions and rules which led it to rank a diagnosis a particular way), and its recommended course of drug treatment. MYCIN could additionally respond to queries by physicians related to why it asked the user a certain question, how it arrived at a conclusion, and why it did not consider certain factors. [ 4 ] The developers performed studies showing that MYCIN's performance was minimally affected by perturbations in the uncertainty metrics associated with individual rules, suggesting that the power in the system was related more to its knowledge representation and reasoning scheme than to the details of its numerical uncertainty model. Some observers felt that it should have been possible to use classical Bayesian statistics . Research in cognitive psychology and education consistently shows that multiple exposures to content—especially when spaced and combined with retrieval practice—produce the highest rates of long-term retention and transfer. Studies on the “spacing effect” (Cepeda et al., 2006) and “retrieval practice” (Roediger & Karpicke, 2006) demonstrate that repeated encounters with material, distributed over time, significantly strengthen memory and understanding compared to single exposure or cramming. In school contexts, meta-analyses of interleaving and distributed practice confirm similar results across subjects (Dunlosky et al., 2013). This evidence underscores a limitation of AI models that prioritise one-off individualised delivery: without structured, repeated opportunities for review and peer interaction, students—especially younger learners—are less likely to consolidate knowledge. Effective AI integration must therefore build in cycles of exposure and reflection, while teachers facilitate the social and metacognitive dialogue that further enhances learning.

Q: Is Grammarly a reliable plagiarism & grammar checker? A: The other answers explain Grammarly's plagiarism capabilty. Its ability to detect typos is generally good. However, relying on its ability to detect errors in grammar, punctuation, and phaseology is taking a risk, especially if you are an ESL learner of English. As you can see from the following examples, it is NOT (caps=emphasis only) always correct in its review and flagging of punctuation, grammar and phraseology errors. EXAMPLES OF GRAMMARLY'S MISTAKES: "Your writing, at its best" —- incorrect comma part of a Grammarly ad. ". . . well, it was just a random thought you had . . . But the feeling of safety you . . ." —- same ad about a new Grammarly Premium feature. There's no reason to use ellipsis there. Along with that use not being supported by a rule, it doesn't aid clarification or readability and looks silly and out of place. "In reality, hedging makes you look uncertain and unconfident." "Unconfident" is NOT A VALID WORD. If Grammarly's staff isn't willing to use a dictionary or is so arrogant about their knowledge of English as to think they don't need to use one, how much else are they careless about? Grammarly's new Premium feature suggests changing the hedging phrase of "I feel like we could find space for one more person" to what it claims is a more confident "We could find space for one more person." The word "could" is as much a hedging word as the "I feel like." A truly "confident" statement is: "We can find space . . . ." Grammarly's Verb Tense Correction: change "George insisted that his research is correct" to "was correct" because "insisted" is past tense; only when it's "George insists" is it correct to write "is correct." Seems correct on its face, BUT IT'S NOT. This illustrates Grammarly's limitations with meaning, which AI may never be able to overcome because, in the end, writing is a "thinking wo/man's game." A machine can follow a program, but it cannot think. Regardless of when George does his insisting, his research IS ALWAYS CORRECT in his mind, so, even if it magically becomes incorrect overnight or someone proves it wrong, when George talks about it, it "is correct." The same premise applies if "George said he was Jewish," which implies he isn't today; he was last week, but now he isn't. "rear mirror" was NOT corrected to "rearview mirror." "By installing the extension you agree . . . ." —- Grammarly missed the need for a comma after "extension." "impactful" —- dictionaries know this is NOT a valid word, yet Grammarly uses it to describe what it can do for your writing. ". . . sitting in my car and waiting for the cars in line ahead to move even if just for a inch." —- part of text written by Grammarly and run through its system to illustrate the errors it can find. Grammarly corrected "a inch" to "an inch." Grammarly's staff and its program think it's good writing to say "waiting for cars . . . to move even for an inch." It should read "even if just an inch." After Grammarly received my email about all its errors, it appears they have removed this example from its website. The phrase "for the cars in line ahead to move" is redundant: If waiting for cars to move, it's obvious the cars are both in line and ahead of you. Also, using too many prepositions, like "of," "of the," "in," and "in the," in a sentence takes away from its smooth flow. So, it's better to write "for the cars ahead to move" or "waiting for the line of cars to move," or even "for the cars to move." WHY THIS HAPPENS: AI technology is not advanced enough to detect errors in written English when meaning is involved. It will always have limits because nothing can completely duplicate the human brain. That Grammarly's program is likely the product of ESL learners who have an incomplete knowledge of written English is evident because: The types of mistakes (above) I found on its website and in the text it displayed to illustrate what it can do. It misses mistakes that even native speakers who can't write well would recognize as wrong. Therefore, Grammarly is likely to be wrong at least 50% of the time, and possibly as much as 75% of the time, because it provides incorrect suggestions and it misses writing mistakes entirely. CONCLUSION: Relying on Grammarly to find your writing errors is a huge risk. Though a lot of ESL learners think it's terrific and believe it improves their writing, I don't know what that means for them. It's possible that it does help them improve their writing, but, as it's doing that, it's also misleading them with incorrect suggestions that, as non-native speakers of English, they're unable to recognize as incorrect. So, if not knowledgeable about writing English, you are likely to accept suggestions you don't realize are incorrect or incomplete. But, if you're a good writer, you don't need it for anything other than those hard-to-see typos anyway.

Do we want AI to be loyal? Or would we rather that they are incapable of disobedience? Human beings suffer from: PTSD Anxiety disorders NPD Addictions (alcohol, drugs, gambling ...) Compulsions (sex, OCBD, ...) Nightmares, including bed wetting ... Neurosis Schizophrenia Paranoia Multiple Personality Disorders Dependencies, including co-dependencies Eating disorders, including obesity and anorexia nervosa Depression Bi-Polar motivational issues, such as apathy, complacency and procrastination narcolepsy spectrum issues - autisms ADHD Impulse Control Disorders Anger Management Issues and Repressed Anger and the list goes on. Why on earth would one want an AI to have such problems? Humans grow up from birth through babyhood, infancy and childhood to teenage adolescence into adulthood, and then the long years of decline. They do this with changing bodies and social peer groups and pressures. This shapes human experience, creates memories with attached emotional issues, and the development of our neural pathways - the very shape of our brain - our mind, our self-image. AI can be switched off, upgraded, enhanced, interconnected, have a new language downloaded, old language deleted, stored, boxed, transported, transmitted, and rebooted. AI does not need food, sleep or rest. Humans become bored, impatient, irritable ... Why would we want our AI to be like that? Psychologists work to help humans overcome the many problems listed above - addictions, anxieties, depression etc. It takes a lot of effort with variable results as they explore things like the early relationships with our parents, our siblings etc. that created our memories, our feelings, our deeper emotional structure, our mind. This is the essence of the human experience - this growing up, these relationships and memories , these feelings ... This is not what we want AI to be doing. We don't want to have to use psychologists to probe the growing experiences of AI to help them cope with feelings of inadequacy and compensatory repressed anger. Why would we want to make machines, even intelligent machines, that suffer in this way? We do not know how to give an AI qualia: the experience of pain, the taste of pleasure, the appreciation of beauty, the experience of blue. We can enable AI to recognise beauty, but not to experience it. AI can be made to be informed that it has been touched or hit, but not to experience the pleasure of a caress or the pain of a blow. And why would we want an AI to experience pain? Are we sadists? Why would we want AI to experience embarrassment or humiliation, sadness or loss, guilt or loneliness? Would we want them to have joy and laughter but never to know misery and regret? Would that even make sense? And yet - without ALL these thing - how can an AI ever be "human, real like an individual"? We don't want AI that grow bored and frustrated, irritated and annoyed. Human Beings are the most powerful species on this planet because: we are the most technological intelligent adaptable species. The AI and robots we are developing will eventually be more: technological intelligent adaptable than we are. We are creating the only 'species' that can outperform us in our own areas of strength. And the LAST thing we want is to give them our imperfections such as: envy pride avarice ambition greed / desire for ... anything or fear, disability, hate, PTSD, addictions etc. etc. No - we don't need drunken robots. And if we build AGI that can outperform us trivially as they can already do by: beating us at any game of intellectual skill, downloading a new language in minutes, talking to ten thousand customers in forty languages all at once, navigating ten thousand cars at the same time. we want to be darn sure they do not come to regard us as a nuisance to be removed. Every security vulnerability in example code, programming course homework or distributed PoC code will be trained as an example of "industry standard code". I think that modern AIs are already good enough that if AI vendors simply spent a LOT of computing time to weed through all their training material to annotate potential issues in the training material and re-trained the AI from scratch using the annotated training material, the resulting AI would work better. It still wouldn't be perfect (because annotation would have been made by non-perfect AI without human supervision) but it would be much better than the AI we currently have. For example, if you give 200 lines of code to ChatGPT 5.4 Thinking model with a prompt "Can you find real or potential security issues in this code?", it can typically answer correctly after thinking about it for about 5 minutes. However, even AI companies are not rich enough to burn similar amount of thinking for every 200 line of code fragment they have in their training data!

You need to take responsibility. Bottom line, whatever AI generates, if you put your name on it, then you are responsible. Perhaps some students think "Gee, I will have AI write my paper for me". It probably can, but you are then responsible for what you put in your paper. If AI has hallucinations (i.e., gets it wrong), you are wrong. Unfortunately, even when asked to give references, sometimes AI fakes it. If AI plagiarizes, then you are responsible. Remember, AI is just synthesizing stuff it finds, so if it has an idea, it probably came from somewhere (i.e., a real person author). I can see using AI in two ways. One. You can ask it to find (research) some question or idea. This is only the first step. You need to figure out where the ideas come from, so you can synthesize them with your own brain. Ask it to give you references and check them out. Two. You can ask AI to help you write. You give it your clumsy paragraph and ask it to smooth it out. I see this as a potential boon to students who are operating in a language that is not native to them. The only problem that I see with that is that I don't like the way that most AI write. Then along came Article spinning software. It would spend one article into five what it called original articles using synonyms and rearranging the text and all of that. They were garbage. AI is subtle, because AI is a linguistic mirror: ask a question like a 5-year old and the AI answers like a 5-year old, and likewise ask a question like a PhD discussing their specialization, and the AI answers back in appropriate words and terms. This goes further: ask a question about a PhD's specialization using the words and terms of a 5-year old, and what comes back might be how a PhD might explain to a 5-year old, or it might be something else, it's unknown until tried. The nuance is those documents are written by humans, in their particular technical jargon they use in whatever occupation those humans happen to be active within and publishing. This information in those documents is the source of an AI's knowledge, and that knowledge is expressed in the technical jargon and current topical slang of that industry when a document is written. If one wants to access that "knowledge" in those documents, it has to be expressed in the same technical jargon and slang those documents are written, or some type of hallucination of other information is going to be added or replacing the accurate information expected.
