"Predict" is a very vague word here. Do we mean predict trends? In a given city there will be X crimes of a certain type in a given year? Do we mean predict victims? Who is most likey to be a victim of crime? Do we mean specific crimes to be commited? This person will rob this bank between these dates? Do we mean predict crimes that have happened? This crime happened, we predict X was the prepetrator responsible? Do we mean to predict is a particular set of of observations constitutes a crime? Do we mean for an individual if they will commit any crime? Do we mean for an individual if they will be arrested for commiting a crime? There are a lot of different uses here - some more contoversial than others. Some I have direct experience of. Running through... Do we mean predict trends? In a given city there will be X crimes of a certain type in a given year? This is just forecasting. Using more data, more variables and more computationally intensive techniques doesn't fundamentally change anything. Its a cornerstone of public policy to predict resource needs. Its unexciting in many ways, but its a great use of ML/AI that can diliver some incremental benefits over previous approaches due to increased accuracy. It happens and has been happening for some time. Do we mean predict victims? Who is most likey to be a victim of crime? Are some people more at risk than others? If we need to allocate police resources to protect some communities (defined geographically, by age, by ethnicity or however we need) can we use AI to do it more efficiently? How do we use AI to minimise the risk to society overall? THis is another good use... but it has a small sliver of conroversy. Any time to alocate resources to one group at the expense of another there are losers as well as winners. An AI is then (even if tangentialy) causing some people to be more exposed to crime. In my eyes it is a valid payoff to protect the more vulnerable at the expense of the less vulnerable and most police services will be trying to prioritise resource allocation anyway and this doesn't change their objectives, but will just achieve them better. Do we mean specific crimes to be commited? This person will rob this bank between these dates? Othere than some very specific examples this is a bit far fetched. The specificity needed for both a person and a crime makes training a model a nightmare. Possibly with very common crimes - the person who shop lifts most days, or the person who gets into a fight every Saturday night, but then the value an AI brings to this problem is limited. We would be likely to have a very biased data set; we don't tend to have crime records for this but rather arrest records or conviction records. If not every criminal is as likey to have been arrested following a crime then this wil be screwy. If it were to work, then it would need the highest level of scrutiny for bias. Any bias in policing and in arrest patterns would be likely to propagate into the model. The kind of profiling to fall out would be problematic. There are also the issues around the actions to be taken - do you surveil the predicted perpetrators? The victims? How accurate do you need to be to warrent allocating a resource this way? A 1% success rate predicting a given crime at a given location on a given day is a remarkable feat - but is it enough to allocate resource by? Do we mean predict crimes that have happened? This crime happened, we predict X was the prepetrator responsible? This is AI supported investigation and used very cautiously, it can be effective. What this can be used for is to provide investigators with a generated list of similar crimes - similar descriptions, similar locations, patterns etc.. It isn't providing the answer, but expediting the investigation by helping to prioritise suspects and giving investigators more information and more relevant information. When it comes to an individual already subject to an investigation, it has little value. An AI is unikely to have a better perspective than a focussed investigative officer - the ability to pull through VAST amounts of data and to do this for a lot of crimes where resource limitations would otherwise be prohibitive. This needs a lot of scrutiny, depending on the decisions being made. Will it determine which crimes are investigated? Will it mean that certain groups of people become much more able to get away with crimes than others? Do we mean to predict if a particular set of of observations constitutes a crime? This is a common enough use case anyway - this is basically fraud detection for things like financial transactions. Getting more and more sophisticated is usually seen as a good thing. Do we mean for an individual if they will commit any crime?/Do we mean for an individual if they will be arrested for commiting a crime? This is close to the (in)famous COMPAS study predicting recidivism amongst offenders. It can be controversial, and like a lot of these things you need to be realy, really careful with training data. Moreover, you need to use it in the circumstances for which it was designed and must remember that rearest is not the same as reoffence. So broadly, I am in favour of using smart approaches in law enforcement but there are a lot of pitfalls here and a lot of safeguards needed. I have built some AI systems for police services and I know they take the ethics very seriously (in the UK at least) which is somewhat reasuring - even so there are still some occasional missteps.

First, my generic answer (specific answer below): The problem with all of these "[when] will Automation (AI, robots, machines, etc.) replace job XXX?" questions is that it implies an "all or nothing" answer... As if one day we'll have humans doing XXX and the next they'll all be replaced by machines and what happens before then isn't important. The way that automation (machines and AI) works is by making people more productive. By doing so a company may need fewer humans to do the same (or more) amount of work; for example an accounting firm may have employed 20 people back in the physical "pen & paper" days... but now do the same work with as few as 5 people using specialized accounting software on a computer. Automation "replaced" 15+ people... but there are still people doing those jobs. The same thing is going to happen with AI. I suspect that 50 years from now there will still be a lot of people still doing the same jobs that people do today... but there may be fewer of them doing it because they'll be using AI to help them be more productive than all the people necessary to do that work now. IOW: instead of asking about automation replacing a job... ask how automation can assist people in performing that job more efficiently. — Now, a specific answer about Sales people: Currently there is an aversion to sales people; most of us would rather go online and research our purchases than have to deal with a sales person (but perhaps I'm projecting). But if you have a "brick and mortar" store you'll most likely have sales people. I don't see these people being "replaced" anytime soon. Note however that long before these people are "replaced" AI will be being used to evaluate customers and inform sales persons as to what strategies might be employed to "make the sale".

Right now, the biggest limitation of artificial intelligence is the data that we use for training. Let's say we train a robot to tie a knot. We feed it data over a period of months on how to tie a knot on a particular type of rope. Let's say that ideally, we would want to robot to tie a knot using a double knot, but all the data we gave it only showed how to tie a single knot. We now want to see how the robot performs on a rope that is roughly similar to the ones that we have trained it on and it turns out that it does a single knot pretty well, but does not know how to tie a double knot. We then see if the robot can at least tie a single knot on a shoe, but it can't do that either because the data we gave it was on a single rope and the robot tries to apply what it learned from training but it does not generalize to the problem of tying a single not on a shoe. So this situation demonstrates some big takeaways of machine learning and AI systems. If we train our models with data that does not reflect our end goal, the model is not going to perform well. If we train our model for a specific instance of the problem, it is still a difficult problem to use these specific instances of the problem to generalize into other versions of the problem. Getting good data is a really big issue in the machine learning community, but recently it has gotten better. The problem of generalization is still an active research topic.

Small data size: If the size of the data is very small (typically less than 1000), it is difficult for even the simplest machine learning model to learn. Especially if the number of attributes (no. of columns) is more than no. of data points (no. of rows). Language: Most classical NLP (natural language processing) techniques use a mostly heuristic driven (grammar rules based) approach for processing text. For e.g. stemming and lemmatization of words which involve converting different parts of speech in their root form (converting slowly to slow) is not a machine learning based approach. Essentially machine learning is useful when you want to predict a conditional variable based on unconditional variables. You would need to analyze the problem and see if that is the case with your problem. If it is not, then maybe machine learning is not suitable. If it is, machine learning should be suitable. However, which machine learning approach and model will work would vary a lot based on the problem.

I think it's the only way for the educational system to provide a meaningful assessment of students' work. This is not a new problem, however. Studies have found that about 60% of university students cheat regularly. And it is very unfair to students who don't cheat. It also gives a very false picture of a student's skills to prospective employers. Students who cheat - whether they are using AI or other humans to do their work - are not learning the material, and yet they can get high grades in the traditional system. I think the solution is pretty straightforward, and it's what I am now doing in the classes I teach: (1). Give assignments as usual. Review them and provide feedback, but don't count them toward the grade. Students can do the assignments and learn from them; they can cheat and have and AI or another person do the work; or they can skip the assignments entirely. It's up to them. (2). Have regular in-class exams. The grade is based on these exams. To prevent cheating, we use something called a "lockdown browser." Once the student starts the exam, they cannot access any other window on their computer. They can't look things up, they can't access AI, they can't message their friends. And of course, phones are prohibited during the exam. If I see a phone, the student gets a 0 on the exam.
Students are being exposed to AI everywhere right now. But exposure is not the same thing as literacy.

The following answer is inspired by Ned Block. New York University, Philosophy: On_a_confusion_about_a_function_of_consc.pdf, page 229. "One can accept the idea of phenomenal consciousness as distinct from any cognitive or functional or intentional notion while frowning on a modular treatment of it." The distinction between ontological phenomenal consciousness and epistemic access consciousness can be exactly what we call cognition and then it makes sense. "Self-access consciousness" may refer to access to entity directives owned by a specific entity. As an alternative "Access consciousness" may refer to access to any chosen set of preferred entity directives. My answer to "Hypothetical: If AI were to become self aware and decide to kill us, couldn't we just EMP them and be done with it?" only considered phenomenal consciousness, while the current answer also includes the distinction between ontological phenomenal consciousness versus epistemic access consciousness. So, as my answer to this question is that ideally we all are gifted with a self-awareness (sic!) that should ensure the ability of an entity to identify a threat of an external unfriendly mind takeover. I think that this ability is crucial to filter contemporary fake news. Compare the Swedish Greta syndrome.

Your average teacher who has a billion and one things to take care of besides "teaching and grading" is not necessarily going to catch AI-generated text if they have dozens or even hundreds of essays to review. The problem is rather obvious: If these kids don't even practice writing, how the hell are they going to get good at it? I'm expecting that in 10–20 years, when Gen Alpha (and late Gen-Z) is finally going to start working, older people like me will probably have to handhold them when it comes to this. Or they just "GPT this, GPT that" in front of their computers. A lot of writing will be the same boring, dull, artificial tone that GPT and others use and us poor bastards will have to religiously double-check everything in case the AI hallucinates and writes nonsense. Maybe even literature is going to take a hit as people start posting tons of slop content (already happening right now, including on Quora) and eventually get so used to it that it becomes the new "standard". Similarly, they'll probably have a hard time understanding longer texts because they're so used to "AI, generate a summary of this novel" or whatever it is the teacher is assigning today. "BuT...bUt thEy SaId thE SamE thIng WiTh cAlcuLators!" In order to use calculators, you need to know what the fuck "multiplication", "division", and such are. With AI, you don't need to know how to write to generate a text.The more I learn about AI, the more I think it really should have been a tool strictly for professionals. Using AI to diagnose breast cancer earlier than possible before? I'm all for that. Use AI to optimize designs from an existing (human-made) "template"? Why not? But by releasing it to the general public, it's been turned into a mental shortcut that can seriously harm the future of the human race. We're already getting sick with obesity, cancer, and all that from our sedentary lifestyle. Imagine that happening with our brains?

If you get output from ChatGPT , or any other AI, and present or publish it as if it was your own work, that is plagiarism. Also, if ChatGPT reproduces recognizable fragments of works it found on the web in its output, and these are not [properly credited when you present the work, that is also plagiarism. It may also, be copyright infringement, the law on that is not yet settled. There is at least one suit over that issue in progress. The general rule is, if work is presented without the source being properly credited, even if the work is long out of copyright, that is plagiarism.
